steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank falls between a pair of commas inside the subject ("the researchers, ____ computer imaging..., aimed to shed light..."). Whatever fills the blank must form a participial phrase that modifies "the researchers" without disrupting the main clause "the researchers... aimed to shed light."

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'using': A present participial phrase — "using computer imaging to compare..." — correctly modifies the subject and is set off by the surrounding commas. The main clause "the researchers... aimed" remains intact. ✓
  • Choice B — 'to use': An infinitive phrase does not function as a participial modifier. Inserting "to use" makes the sentence read as if the researchers exist "in order to use" imaging, which is not the meaning and creates an awkward structure between the commas. ✗
  • Choice C — 'use': A bare verb between the commas creates a second finite verb ("researchers use... aimed"), making the sentence a comma-splice or a structural error. ✗
  • Choice D — 'used': A past-tense verb has the same problem as C — it would read as a second main clause rather than a modifier, resulting in an ill-formed sentence. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
